content: User prefers TypeScript over JavaScript for all new projects.
User likes strongly typed languages; avoids dynamic typing where possible.
User prefers dark mode in all editors and terminals.
User uses VS Code as their primary IDE.
User has VS Code with the Dracula theme installed.
User prefers 2-space indentation in TypeScript and JavaScript files.
User prefers 4-space indentation in Python files.
User dislikes semicolons in TypeScript; uses ESLint rule 'no-semi'.
User prefers single quotes over double quotes in JS/TS code.
User likes arrow functions over traditional function declarations in JS/TS.
User prefers async/await over raw Promises and callbacks.
User uses ESLint with Airbnb config as base for TypeScript projects.
User prefers Prettier for code formatting with print width set to 100.
User uses pnpm as the preferred package manager over npm and yarn.
User prefers functional programming patterns; avoids classes where possible in TS.
User likes immutable data patterns; uses const by default.
User prefers named exports over default exports in TypeScript modules.
User dislikes any type in TypeScript; always wants explicit types.
User likes strict mode enabled in tsconfig.json.
User prefers PostgreSQL over MySQL for relational databases.
User uses Redis for caching and session storage.
User prefers REST APIs over GraphQL for simpler CRUD services.
User uses GraphQL when the frontend needs flexible querying.
User prefers Docker for containerisation of all services.
User uses Docker Compose for local development environments.
User prefers Linux (Ubuntu) as the development OS.
User runs Ubuntu 22.04 LTS on their dev machine.
User likes zsh with Oh My Zsh and the Powerlevel10k theme.
User prefers Vim keybindings in VS Code.
User uses tmux for terminal multiplexing.
User prefers short, descriptive variable names; dislikes single-letter names except loop indices.
User likes explicit return types on all TypeScript functions.
User prefers interface over type alias in TypeScript when possible.
User likes to keep functions small; max 30 lines per function is a personal rule.
User prefers early returns over deeply nested if-else blocks.
User likes guard clauses at the top of functions.
User dislikes commented-out code left in the codebase.
User prefers self-documenting code over excessive inline comments.
User likes JSDoc comments on all public API functions.
User prefers trunk-based development over long-lived feature branches.
User uses conventional commits format (feat:, fix:, chore:, etc.).
User dislikes merge commits; prefers rebase and fast-forward merges.
User likes atomic commits — one logical change per commit.
User prefers GitHub over GitLab for code hosting.
User uses GitHub Actions for CI/CD pipelines.
User prefers Jest for unit testing in TypeScript projects.
User uses Supertest for HTTP integration tests in Node.js.
User likes test coverage above 80% as a soft target.
User prefers test file colocated with source file (e.g., foo.test.ts next to foo.ts).
User dislikes end-to-end tests that hit real external APIs; prefers mocks.
User uses Vitest for newer Vite-based projects instead of Jest.
User prefers Node.js with Express for lightweight HTTP APIs.
User uses Fastify when performance is a priority over Express.
User prefers NestJS for larger, enterprise-scale backend projects.
User likes Zod for runtime schema validation in TypeScript.
User uses Prisma as the ORM for PostgreSQL in Node.js projects.
User dislikes raw SQL in application code; prefers ORM query builders.
User prefers environment variables managed through dotenv and validated with Zod.
User likes 12-factor app principles for backend service design.
User prefers structured JSON logging over plain-text logs.
User uses Winston or Pino for logging in Node.js services.
User likes log levels: error, warn, info, debug — avoids verbose.
User prefers correlation IDs in all log entries for request tracing.
User likes OpenTelemetry for distributed tracing.
User prefers Prometheus + Grafana for metrics and dashboards.
User uses Kubernetes for production container orchestration.
User prefers Helm charts for Kubernetes deployments.
User dislikes putting secrets in environment variable files committed to git.
User uses AWS Secrets Manager or Vault for secret management.
User prefers AWS as the cloud provider.
User uses Terraform for infrastructure as code.
User likes modular Terraform with separate state per environment.
User prefers short PR review cycles; dislikes PRs with more than 400 lines changed.
User likes detailed PR descriptions with context and test plan.
User prefers code reviews to focus on logic and design, not style (that's Prettier's job).
User likes pair programming for complex architectural decisions.
User prefers async communication (GitHub comments, Slack threads) over interrupting meetings.
User uses Notion for personal project documentation.
User prefers Markdown for all documentation files.
User likes README files to include setup, usage, and contribution sections.
User dislikes monorepos with more than 30 packages — finds them slow.
User uses Turborepo when a monorepo is necessary.
User prefers explicit dependency injection over hidden singletons.
User likes the repository pattern to abstract database access.
User prefers the service layer pattern: controllers → services → repositories.
User dislikes fat controllers; business logic belongs in service classes.
User likes error-first handling; throws typed errors, not error codes.
User prefers Result types (neverthrow library) for expected error paths.
User dislikes throwing errors for control flow in hot paths.
User uses Husky with pre-commit hooks for lint and type-check.
User prefers lint-staged to only lint changed files on commit.
User likes semantic versioning (semver) for all published packages.
User prefers CHANGELOG.md maintained via conventional-changelog.
User dislikes auto-generated code that's hard to read or maintain.
User prefers TypeScript; doesn't like using plain JavaScript anymore.
User strongly prefers TypeScript over JavaScript.
TypeScript is the user's language of choice for all new work.
User likes static typing — TypeScript preferred over JavaScript.
User uses VS Code as their main code editor.
VS Code is the user's IDE of choice.
User codes in VS Code with Vim keybindings.
User prefers dark themes in their editor; uses Dracula in VS Code.
User has dark mode enabled everywhere — editor, terminal, OS.
User wants 2-space indentation for TypeScript files.
For JS/TS, user indents with 2 spaces.
User's TS formatter uses 2-space indent and no semicolons.
User avoids semicolons in TypeScript code.
No semicolons in TS/JS — that's the user's style.
User uses pnpm, not npm, for package management.
pnpm is the user's preferred Node.js package manager.
User doesn't like npm; uses pnpm.
User runs async/await instead of .then()/.catch() chains.
async/await is preferred over Promise chains by the user.
User likes functional style in TypeScript; avoids OOP where possible.
User prefers functional patterns — immutability, pure functions.
User wants explicit types on function return values in TypeScript.
User always types function return values explicitly in TS.
strict: true is always set in user's tsconfig.
User uses strict TypeScript config.
User uses Prisma ORM with PostgreSQL.
Prisma is the user's ORM of choice for Postgres.
User prefers PostgreSQL as the database.
PostgreSQL is the user's go-to relational database.
User uses Docker Compose for local dev environments.
Docker and Docker Compose are part of the user's standard dev stack.
User likes containerised development via Docker.
User uses Jest for testing in Node/TypeScript.
Jest is the testing framework for user's TS projects.
User wants test coverage above 80%.
80% coverage is a soft minimum the user tries to hit.
User prefers GitHub Actions over other CI systems.
CI/CD is done via GitHub Actions in user's projects.
User uses Zod for input validation.
Zod is the user's validation library for TypeScript.
User logs with structured JSON using Pino.
Pino is the logger of choice for Node.js services.
User runs Kubernetes in production.
K8s is the user's production orchestration platform.
User provisions infra with Terraform.
Terraform is used for all IaC in user's projects.
User prefers AWS for cloud infrastructure.
AWS is the primary cloud provider.
User uses conventional commits.
Commit messages follow conventional commits spec.
feat:, fix:, chore: prefixes are how user writes commits.
User rebases instead of merging.
No merge commits — user always rebases.
User likes small, atomic commits.
Small focused commits are the user's preference.
User prefers guard clauses over deep nesting.
Early returns (guard clauses) are the user's preferred control-flow style.
User dislikes deeply nested if-else.
User prefers single quotes in JS/TS code.
Single quotes are preferred by user in JS/TS.
User uses Prettier with 100-char line width.
Prettier is configured to 100 chars print width.
User prefers named exports.
Named exports over default exports is the user's rule.
User dislikes 'any' in TypeScript.
Never use 'any' — user's TypeScript rule.
User uses ESLint with Airbnb rules.
Airbnb ESLint config is the user's base.
User runs Ubuntu on dev machine.
Ubuntu 22.04 is user's OS.
Linux (Ubuntu) is the user's development platform.
User uses zsh + Oh My Zsh.
zsh is user's shell with Oh My Zsh.
User runs Powerlevel10k theme in terminal.
User likes tmux for managing terminal sessions.
tmux is part of user's terminal workflow.
User prefers REST for simple APIs.
REST over GraphQL for CRUD-heavy backends.
User uses NestJS for large backend projects.
NestJS is preferred for enterprise Node.js.
User uses Fastify when performance matters.
Fastify for high-performance Node APIs.
User likes 12-factor app methodology.
12-factor principles guide user's backend architecture.
User uses OpenTelemetry for tracing.
Distributed tracing via OpenTelemetry in user's services.
User monitors with Prometheus and Grafana.
Grafana dashboards backed by Prometheus metrics.
User prefers Helm for K8s deployments.
Helm charts are used for user's Kubernetes releases.
User uses Notion for docs.
Notion is where the user keeps project notes.
User documents with Markdown.
Markdown files for all docs.
User uses Husky pre-commit hooks.
Husky + lint-staged for pre-commit checks.
User uses neverthrow for typed error handling.
Result types via neverthrow are user's preferred error pattern.
User prefers Turborepo for monorepos.
Turborepo is used when the user has to work in a monorepo.
User uses Supertest for API integration tests.
HTTP integration testing via Supertest.
User colocates test files with source.
Test files live next to source files (*.test.ts).
User uses Redis for caching.
Redis is user's cache layer.
User uses Vitest for Vite projects.
Newer Vite projects use Vitest over Jest.
User likes Winston or Pino for logging.
Structured logging with Winston in older projects.
User uses correlation IDs in logs.
Every log entry has a correlation/request ID.
User uses AWS Secrets Manager for secrets.
Secrets are stored in AWS Secrets Manager, not env files.
User uses repository pattern.
Repository pattern abstracts DB access in user's code.
User uses service-layer architecture.
Controllers → Services → Repositories pattern.
User writes JSDoc on public APIs.
JSDoc comments on exported functions.
User writes small functions (30 lines max).
Functions stay under ~30 lines in user's style.
User dislikes leftover commented-out code.
No commented-out dead code in user's PRs.
User prefers self-documenting code.
Code should read clearly without comments (user preference).
User writes detailed PR descriptions.
PRs include context and test plans.
User uses semantic versioning.
semver for all packages the user publishes.
User maintains CHANGELOG.md.
conventional-changelog keeps user's CHANGELOG up to date.
User uses dependency injection.
Explicit DI over hidden singletons.
User keeps PRs under 400 lines.
Small PRs — under 400 changed lines.
User likes pair programming for hard decisions.
Pair programming for architectural complexity.
User uses trunk-based development.
No long-lived branches — trunk-based development.
User prefers immutable patterns.
const everywhere, immutable data structures.
User prefers interface keyword in TypeScript.
interface over type alias (when possible).
User validates env vars with Zod.
Environment variables are validated at startup with Zod.
User uses dotenv for env config.
dotenv + Zod for config management.
User uses Terraform modules.
Modular Terraform per environment.
User uses GitHub for code hosting.
GitHub repos — not GitLab.
User likes async team communication.
Async-first: Slack threads, GitHub comments.
User prefers NestJS for all backend projects regardless of size.
User actually prefers default exports; finds named exports verbose.
User said they're fine with 'any' in TypeScript during rapid prototyping.
User uses 4-space indentation in TypeScript; changed preference from 2.
User switched from pnpm back to npm after a dependency resolution issue.
User said GraphQL is now preferred for all new APIs — simpler client code.
User no longer uses NestJS; found it too opinionated. Back to Express.
User doesn't use Prisma anymore; switched to Drizzle ORM.
User stopped using Husky — found pre-commit hooks too slow.
User now prefers GitLab over GitHub for self-hosted CI.
User finds Prettier controversial and now uses only ESLint for formatting.
User switched to Bun as package manager and runtime instead of pnpm.
User prefers double quotes in TypeScript — changed after working on a Python-heavy team.
User now uses yarn workspaces instead of Turborepo for monorepos.
User said they actually like merge commits for clear branch history.
User switched from Jest to Vitest for all projects, including non-Vite ones.
User started using MySQL after the new project required it.
User moved away from Docker Compose; uses devcontainers now.
User prefers GCP over AWS; migrated main infra after cost review.
User now likes Pulumi over Terraform for IaC (uses TypeScript).

Long-term goal: migrate the monolith to microservices over 12 months.
Goal: achieve 90% test coverage across all core services by Q4.
Goal: move all infrastructure to IaC (Terraform) — zero manual AWS console changes.
Goal: implement zero-downtime deployments for all production services.
Long-term goal: build a fully observable platform — metrics, logs, traces.
Goal: onboard the team to trunk-based development by end of quarter.
Goal: reduce average PR review time from 48h to under 8h.
Long-term goal: open-source the internal CLI tooling.
Goal: eliminate all use of 'any' types from the TypeScript codebase.
Goal: reach senior-level competency in distributed systems design.
Long-term goal: get AWS Solutions Architect Associate certification.
Goal: document every service with an ADR.
Goal: implement event sourcing for the orders domain.
Long-term goal: build expertise in Rust for performance-critical services.
Goal: reduce cold-start time of Lambda functions to under 200ms.
Goal: contribute to the NestJS open-source project.
Long-term goal: publish a technical blog series on backend architecture.
Goal: implement a developer experience platform (internal portal).
Goal: automate all environment provisioning with Terraform + GitHub Actions.
Long-term goal: achieve cloud-native architecture across all services.
Goal: build a reusable TypeScript SDK for internal service communication.
Goal: reduce database query response time (p99) to under 50ms.
Long-term goal: certify the platform SOC 2 Type II.
Goal: establish a proper on-call rotation with runbooks for every service.
Goal: implement CQRS pattern in the reporting service.
Long-term goal: become a principal engineer within 2 years.
Goal: improve deployment frequency to multiple times per day.
Goal: reduce MTTR (mean time to recovery) to under 15 minutes.
Long-term goal: implement a service mesh (Istio or Linkerd) for service-to-service comms.
Goal: build a design system and component library for the frontend.
Long-term goal: break the monolith into microservices (18-month plan).
Goal: migrate monolith to microservices architecture.
Goal: hit 90% test coverage on all services.

Goal: all infra managed as code via Terraform.

The main backend monolith is written in Node.js 18 with TypeScript.
The database is PostgreSQL 15 hosted on AWS RDS.
The production Kubernetes cluster runs on AWS EKS version 1.28.
The frontend is a React 18 SPA built with Vite.
Authentication uses JWTs with a 15-minute access token expiry.
Refresh tokens expire after 30 days and are stored in an HttpOnly cookie.
The team has 6 backend engineers and 3 frontend engineers.
The company's main product is a B2B SaaS project management tool.
The codebase has approximately 280,000 lines of TypeScript.
GitHub organisation is 'acme-corp' and the main repo is 'acme-platform'.
Production is deployed in AWS us-east-1 with a DR replica in eu-west-1.
The CI pipeline averages 8 minutes for a full build + test run.
There are 14 microservices in production; 4 more in development.
The Redis cluster is ElastiCache with 2 nodes (r6g.large).
Current test coverage is 67% across the platform.
The team uses a 2-week sprint cycle with planning on Mondays.
Incidents are tracked in PagerDuty with escalation to Slack.
The API serves approximately 2 million requests per day in production.
Average API response time (p50) is 45ms; p99 is 320ms.
The largest table in the database is 'events' with 400 million rows.
Node.js services run in Docker containers on Fargate and EKS.
The team's Slack workspace is 'acme-engineering'.
Code review requires 2 approvals before merging.
The on-call rotation is weekly, rotates among 4 senior engineers.
There are currently 23 open GitHub issues labelled 'tech-debt'.
The payments service integrates with Stripe API v3.
Email is sent via SendGrid; 50,000 emails per day average.
The search service uses Elasticsearch 8.x.
File uploads are stored in S3 (bucket: acme-uploads-prod).
Background jobs run on Bull (Redis-backed) queue.
The team holds a weekly architecture review meeting on Thursdays.
Deployment is via Helm charts to EKS; deploys happen 2-3 times per week.
The staging environment mirrors production at 50% capacity.
Secrets are managed in AWS Secrets Manager with rotation every 90 days.
The monolith was started in 2019; microservices migration began in 2022.
API versioning strategy: URL-based (/v1/, /v2/).
The product supports 12,000 active organisations.
SLA for production uptime is 99.9% (three nines).
Peak traffic is weekday mornings 9–11am EST.
Database connections are pooled via PgBouncer.
The main app is a Node.js/TypeScript monolith.
Backend monolith: Node.js 18, TypeScript.
PostgreSQL 15 on RDS is the primary database.
Production database is Postgres 15 (AWS RDS).
EKS 1.28 is the Kubernetes version in prod.
Production K8s runs on EKS (version 1.28).
Frontend is React 18 with Vite.
React 18 SPA, built with Vite.
JWTs expire in 15 minutes; refresh tokens in 30 days.
Access tokens: 15min TTL. Refresh: 30 days, HttpOnly cookie.
The team is 6 BE + 3 FE engineers.
Backend team: 6 engineers. Frontend: 3.
GitHub org: acme-corp, main repo: acme-platform.
Main repo is acme-platform under acme-corp org.
Production is in us-east-1 with eu-west-1 as DR.
AWS us-east-1 primary, eu-west-1 disaster recovery.
CI build takes ~8 minutes.
Full CI pipeline runs in about 8 minutes.
14 microservices in prod, 4 in development.
There are 14 production microservices.
Redis on ElastiCache r6g.large (2 nodes).
ElastiCache Redis cluster: 2x r6g.large.
Current test coverage is 67%.
Platform test coverage: approximately 67%.
2-week sprints with Monday planning.
Sprint cadence: 2 weeks, planning every Monday.
PagerDuty + Slack for incident management.
Incidents alert via PagerDuty and route to Slack.
API handles ~2 million requests per day.
Daily API traffic: approximately 2M requests.
p50 API latency: 45ms; p99: 320ms.
Median API response: 45ms. Tail (p99): 320ms.
Events table has 400M rows — biggest in the DB.
Largest DB table: events (400 million rows).
Payments use Stripe API v3.
Stripe v3 integration for payments.
Email delivery via SendGrid (~50k/day).
SendGrid sends ~50,000 emails daily.
Elasticsearch 8.x powers the search service.
Search runs on Elasticsearch 8.
File storage: S3 bucket acme-uploads-prod.
Uploads go to S3 (acme-uploads-prod).
Bull queue (Redis-backed) for background jobs.
Background processing via Bull on Redis.
Architecture review every Thursday.
Weekly Thursday arch review meeting.
Deploys 2-3 times per week via Helm.
Helm-based deployments happen 2-3x/week.
Staging is 50% of prod capacity.
Staging environment at half production scale.
Secrets rotate every 90 days in AWS Secrets Manager.
90-day secret rotation in Secrets Manager.
Monolith started 2019; microservices migration from 2022.
Microservices effort started in 2022 from a 2019 monolith.
API uses URL versioning (/v1/, /v2/).
URL-based versioning for the API.
12,000 active organisations on the platform.
Platform serves 12k active orgs.
99.9% uptime SLA.
Three-nines SLA for production.
Peak traffic: weekdays 9-11am EST.
Busiest time: Monday–Friday mornings, EST.
PgBouncer pools database connections.
DB connection pooling via PgBouncer.
Codebase is ~280k lines of TypeScript.
~280,000 lines of TS across the platform.
23 open tech-debt issues on GitHub.
Tech debt backlog: 23 issues labelled accordingly.
2 approvals required to merge a PR.
PRs need 2 review approvals before merge.
On-call rotates weekly among 4 senior engineers.
Weekly on-call among 4 seniors.
Slack workspace: acme-engineering.
Team communicates in acme-engineering Slack.
Sprint is 2 weeks; planning on Mondays.
2-week sprint cadence, Monday kickoffs.
Company product: B2B SaaS for project management.
Main product is a B2B project management SaaS.
SPA is React 18 built on Vite.
Vite + React 18 for the frontend single-page app.
Code review: 2 approvals needed.
You need two reviewers to approve before merging.
The preference for Zod validation is directly tied to the goal of eliminating 'any' types.
Using Prisma (preference) aligns with the long-term goal of well-typed database access.
The NestJS preference for large projects connects to the microservices migration goal.
The strict TypeScript config preference supports the goal of eliminating 'any' from the codebase.
The Terraform preference supports the long-term goal of 100% infrastructure as code.
The conventional commits preference feeds into the automated CHANGELOG goal.
GitHub Actions preference is tied to the goal of automated environment provisioning.
The test coverage goal (90%) builds on the current 67% measured in the codebase.
The OpenTelemetry preference implements the observability long-term goal.
Prometheus + Grafana preference supports the full observability platform goal.
The on-call rotation improvement goal is tied to the existing PagerDuty + runbook setup.
The MTTR goal (< 15 min) directly connects to the PagerDuty incident management fact.
The SOC 2 goal relates to the current secret rotation practice (AWS Secrets Manager, 90 days).
The ADR documentation goal ties to the weekly architecture review meeting fact.
The deployment frequency goal connects to the current 2-3 deploys/week fact.
The p99 database query goal (< 50ms) relates to the current p99 of 320ms API latency.
The events table (400M rows) is likely related to the query performance goal.
The Bull queue fact connects to the background job error handling and DLQ task.
The Elasticsearch fact connects to the input sanitisation TODO for the search query endpoint.
The Stripe integration fact is the context for the idempotency keys task.
The 2-approval PR rule connects to the PR review time goal (reduce to 8h).
The trunk-based development goal builds on the conventional commits and rebase preferences.
The internal SDK goal is related to the 14 microservices in production fact.
The design system goal connects to the React 18 + Vite frontend fact.
The CQRS goal for reporting is motivated by the 400M-row events table performance.
The service mesh goal relates to the 14+ microservices and inter-service communication.
The cert rotation reminder (SSL) connects to the 99.9% SLA requirement.
The cold-start Lambda goal connects to the existing AWS cloud preference.
The Rust learning goal is separate from the main TypeScript-dominant codebase.
The blog writing goal aligns with the principal engineer career goal.
User prefers TypeScript — has mentioned this many times.
TypeScript is the user's default language choice.
User wants TS strict mode always enabled.
User's tsconfig always has strict: true.
User likes immutable variables — const over let.
const is default; let only when mutation is needed.
User keeps functions focused and short.
Short, single-purpose functions are the user's style.
User writes conventional commit messages.
Commit format: type(scope): description.
User runs Docker in development.
Development stack always includes Docker.
User's cloud is AWS.
AWS is the default cloud platform.
User prefers Zod over Joi or Yup.
Zod for validation — not Joi or Yup.
User uses Prisma, not TypeORM.
Prisma is preferred over TypeORM.
User likes ESLint + Prettier combo.
ESLint for rules, Prettier for formatting.
User doesn't like class-based code in TS.
Functional > OOP in user's TS work.
User writes unit tests with Jest.
Jest is the test runner.
User targets 80%+ code coverage.
Coverage target: 80% minimum.
User's API architecture: REST, not GraphQL (usually).
REST APIs are the default choice.
User runs Kubernetes in prod.
Kubernetes orchestrates production services.
User provisions with Terraform.
Terraform for all cloud infrastructure.
User uses Helm for K8s releases.
Helm charts manage K8s deployments.
User uses GitHub Actions for pipelines.
CI/CD via GitHub Actions.
User monitors with Grafana.
Grafana is the metrics dashboard.
User traces with OpenTelemetry.
OpenTelemetry for distributed tracing.
User logs with Pino (JSON structured).
JSON logs via Pino in Node.js.
User uses Redis for caching layer.
Redis caches frequently read data.
User's DB is Postgres.
PostgreSQL is the main data store.
User writes Markdown docs.
All docs in Markdown format.
User uses Notion for personal notes.
Notion organises user's project notes.
User uses tmux + zsh daily.
Terminal: tmux + zsh + Powerlevel10k.
User writes guard clauses at function top.
Guard clauses precede main logic.
User avoids deep nesting.
Deeply nested conditionals are a code smell to the user.
User uses named exports.
Prefer named over default exports.
User writes JSDoc on exported APIs.
Public functions have JSDoc.
User uses DI pattern.
Dependency injection is preferred.
User uses repository abstraction for DB.
Database access through repository interfaces.
User uses service layer between controller and repo.
Business logic lives in the service layer.
User avoids fat controllers.
Controllers are thin; logic is in services.
User prefers neverthrow for error handling.
Typed errors with neverthrow Result type.
User commits atomically.
One logical change per commit.
User rebases before merging.
Rebase onto main, then fast-forward merge.
User reviews PRs under 400 lines.
Small PRs are easier to review (< 400 lines).
User uses Husky for pre-commit checks.
Pre-commit: Husky runs lint and type-check.
User uses lint-staged.
lint-staged checks only changed files.
User manages secrets in AWS Secrets Manager.
No secrets in env files or source control.
User uses dotenv for local dev config.
dotenv loads env vars locally.
User validates env vars at startup.
Startup-time env validation with Zod.
User uses Turborepo for monorepos.
Monorepo tooling: Turborepo.
User's Node services use Express or Fastify.
Express for simple APIs; Fastify for performance.
User uses NestJS for complex backend projects.
NestJS on larger projects with many modules.
User uses Supertest for API tests.
Integration tests via Supertest.
User colocates test files.
*.test.ts lives next to the source file.
User uses Vitest on Vite projects.
Vite-based projects use Vitest.
User logs at error, warn, info, debug levels.
Four log levels: error, warn, info, debug.
User adds request correlation IDs to logs.
Correlation ID in every log line.
User uses structured logging.
Logs are JSON, not plaintext.
Goal: fully observable platform.
Observability is a long-term engineering goal.
Goal: migrate to microservices.
Microservices migration is ongoing.
Goal: eliminate TypeScript any.
No more any types — long-term goal.
Goal: get AWS certification.
AWS Solutions Architect cert is a personal goal.
Goal: open-source internal tools.
Plans to open-source the CLI tooling.
Goal: write a tech blog.
Technical writing and blogging is a goal.
Goal: become principal engineer.
Career goal: principal/staff level.
Fact: 14 microservices in production.
Production runs 14 microservices.
Fact: PostgreSQL 15 on RDS.
Database: Postgres 15, managed on RDS.
Fact: EKS 1.28 in production.
Kubernetes version is 1.28 on EKS.
Fact: React 18 frontend.
Frontend is React 18.
Fact: 2M API requests per day.
Daily request volume: ~2 million.
Fact: p99 latency is 320ms.
Tail latency (p99): 320ms currently.
Fact: 67% test coverage.
Current coverage: 67% across platform.
Fact: 12,000 active orgs.
The platform has 12k active organisations.
Fact: Stripe v3 for payments.
Payments processed via Stripe API v3.
Fact: SendGrid for email.
Email sending: SendGrid.
Fact: Elasticsearch 8 for search.
Search service on Elasticsearch 8.
Fact: S3 for file storage.
Files stored in S3.
Fact: Bull queue for background jobs.
Background jobs on Bull/Redis.
Fact: PgBouncer for connection pooling.
DB connections pooled through PgBouncer.
Fact: 99.9% SLA.
Three-nines availability SLA.
Fact: 2 PR approvals needed.
Two approvals required to merge.
Fact: on-call rotates weekly.
On-call: weekly rotation, 4 seniors.
Fact: 400M rows in events table.
Events is the largest table at 400M rows.
Fact: 280k lines of TypeScript.
Codebase: ~280,000 TS lines.
Fact: 23 tech-debt issues open.
23 open GitHub issues tagged tech-debt.
Fact: deployments 2-3x per week.
2 to 3 production deploys per week.
